deck.title: Math Knowledge — Geometry
deck.description: Area, volume, angle, and triangle facts you'll need for the ASVAB MK subtest.

cards.explanation: A square is the special case where length = width, so A = s².

cards.front: Sum of interior angles in any triangle?
cards.explanation: True for every triangle — if you know two angles, subtract their sum from 180° to find the third.
π is the ratio of a circle's circumference to its diameter — same for every circle.

cards.front: Perimeter of a square with side s?
cards.explanation: All four sides are equal, so add s four times — distinct from area (s²) and from the rectangle perimeter formula.

cards.front: Area of a square with side s?
cards.explanation: Just length × width where both sides equal s — don't confuse with perimeter (4s).
Equivalent to 2L + 2W — add all four sides; opposite sides are equal.

cards.front: Area of a circle with radius r?
cards.explanation: Square the radius first, then multiply by π — common mistake is to use diameter instead of radius.

cards.front: Two angles are complementary if their sum is...?
cards.explanation: Complementary = Corner (90°); the alphabetical pairing with supplementary helps you remember the order.

cards.front: Two angles are supplementary if their sum is...?
cards.explanation: Supplementary = Straight line (180°) — two angles on a straight line are always supplementary.

cards.front: Diameter of a circle in terms of radius?
cards.explanation: The diameter passes through the center and equals twice the radius.

cards.front: Volume of a cube with side s?
cards.explanation: Length × width × height where all three are s — units are cubic (e.g., cm³).
Generalizes the cube formula — when l = w = h = s, this collapses to V = s³.
The ½ comes from the fact that a triangle is half a parallelogram with the same base and height.
Only works for right triangles; c is always the hypotenuse (longest side, opposite the right angle).
Both forms are equal because diameter = 2r — pick whichever matches the given measurement.
Use the perpendicular height, NOT the slanted side — same formula as a rectangle since you can rearrange it into one.
Average the two parallel bases, then multiply by the perpendicular height between them.
It's the area of the circular base (πr²) times the height — same pattern as any prism: base area × height.
Spotting these saves time — if two sides match a known triple, the third is automatic, no calculation needed.

cards.front: What's true about vertical angles (formed by two intersecting lines)?
cards.explanation: The two pairs of opposite (across-the-X) angles formed by intersecting lines always have equal measures.
Shortest side is opposite 30°; hypotenuse (opposite 90°) is twice the shortest — middle side equals shortest × √3.

cards.front: Volume of a sphere with radius r?
cards.explanation: Cube the radius, then multiply by 4π/3 — don't confuse with surface area (4πr²).

cards.front: Side ratio of a 45-45-90 (isosceles right) triangle?
cards.explanation: Both legs are equal; the hypotenuse is a leg times √2 — comes straight from a² + a² = c², so c = a√2.
